title: Apostille Documents in the Netherlands
metaTitle: Apostille Documents Netherlands | How to Get an Apostille
subtitle: Learn when an apostille is required for official documents used in the Netherlands and how to obtain one for visas, municipality registration, and other legal processes.
description: Learn how apostille documents work in the Netherlands, when you need an apostille, how to obtain one, typical costs, timelines, and how apostilles are used for visas, municipality registration, and official administration.

image.alt: An expat meticulously reviews official documents at a desk overlooking a picturesque Dutch canal, with traditional brick buildings and bicycles. A laptop screen displays 'Apostille Document' as a completed step, alongside physical books labeled 'Apostille,' 'Legalisation,' and 'Translation,' a passport, and a 'Document Translation Checklist' notebook, symbolizing the process of authenticating documents for life in the Netherlands.

heroCta.title: Get your documents in order
heroCta.supportingText: Use the Document Readiness Checker and Document Legalization guide to see what you need and when.

howToSteps.name: Obtain the original official document
howToSteps.text: Get the certified or original document from the issuing authority in the country where it was issued.

howToSteps.name: Have the document verified
howToSteps.text: Have the document verified by the relevant authority (e.g. notary or issuing body) if required for your country.

howToSteps.name: Apply for apostille
howToSteps.text: Apply for apostille at the designated authority—in the Netherlands, a district court; abroad, the competent authority for that country (e.g. Secretary of State, MEA).

quickAnswers.label: APOSTILLE
quickAnswers.value: International certificate verifying authenticity of a document

quickAnswers.label: HAGUE CONVENTION
quickAnswers.value: Used between countries that signed the Apostille Convention

quickAnswers.label: ISSUED BY COURTS
quickAnswers.value: In the Netherlands, apostilles are issued by district courts

sections.heading: Introduction
body: Many official documents used for immigration or administration in the Netherlands must be authenticated.
This authentication is often done using an apostille certificate, which confirms the authenticity of a document so it can be recognised internationally.
An apostille simplifies the process of recognising foreign documents between countries that signed the Hague Apostille Convention.

links.label: IND – Translation and legalisation of documents

sections.heading: What an apostille is
body: An apostille is an official certificate attached to a document confirming the authenticity of the signature and issuing authority.
This certificate allows the document to be accepted in another country that participates in the Apostille Convention.
In the Netherlands, apostilles are typically issued by district courts.

links.label: Netherlands Worldwide – what is legalisation and apostille

sections.heading: Apostille vs legalisation
body: Two main ways documents are authenticated: Apostille (used between Hague Convention countries; faster and simpler) and full legalisation (used when countries are not part of the convention; may require multiple authorities).
Documents are usually validated with a stamp or sticker during this process.

rows: Single step: one stamp or sticker from designated authority
Multiple steps: often ministry then embassy or consulate
When the issuing country is not in the Hague Convention
Often a few days to a few weeks

links.label: IND – translation and legalisation of documents

sections.heading: When expats need an apostille
body: Many expats must provide apostilled civil documents such as birth or marriage certificates when registering or applying for permits.

sections.heading: Common documents requiring apostille
body: These document types are often required with apostille for use in the Netherlands. Whether apostille or full legalisation applies depends on the country that issued the document.

sections.heading: How to obtain an apostille
body: In the Netherlands, you can request an apostille from a district court. You may apply yourself or have someone do it on your behalf.
Legalisation or apostille is usually completed in the country where the document was issued.
steps: Have the document verified by the relevant authority
Apply for apostille at the designated authority (e.g. district court in the Netherlands, or the competent authority in the country of issue)

sections.heading: Costs and timelines
body: Government apostille fees and processing times vary by country and authority. The figures below are indicative; always confirm with the authority that will legalise your document.

rows: May apply if using intermediaries or couriers
Same day to several days depending on authority

callout.title: Start early
callout.text: Apostille and legalisation can take from a few days to several weeks depending on the country. Start as soon as you know which documents you need.

sections.heading: Country-specific examples
body: Procedures differ per country. Foreign documents used in the Netherlands usually need apostille or legalisation in the country where they were issued. Below are examples; always check the official Netherlands Worldwide page for the country that issued your document.

sections.heading: Common mistakes
body: Avoid these pitfalls to save time and avoid rework.
bullets: Starting translation before apostille when the correct order is apostille first
Using copies instead of originals where originals are required
Assuming apostille and translation are the same (they are different steps)
Waiting too late to start the process

callout.title: Order matters
callout.text: Translating first when apostille should happen first is a common mistake. Check the official steps for the country that issued your document.

sections.heading: Official sources
body: Always confirm requirements with the authority that will receive your document (IND, municipality, university). Use these official sources for apostille and legalisation.

links.label: Netherlands Worldwide – what is legalisation and apostille
IND – translation and legalisation of documents

documentLegalizationCountryExamples.summary: Birth certificate (and other civil records) require apostille from the Secretary of State in the state that issued the document.
workflow: Obtain certified copy of the document from the issuing authority
Request apostille from the competent state Secretary of State (or federal office for federal documents)
Submit apostilled document (and sworn translation if required) to Dutch authorities

documentLegalizationCountryExamples.summary: Documents require apostille from the Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) or the designated state authority.
workflow: Get document attested by the relevant state or notary if required
Submit for apostille at Ministry of External Affairs (or state-level designated authority)
Use apostilled document for Dutch procedures; add sworn translation in the Netherlands if needed

documentLegalizationCountryExamples.summary: Documents require apostille in Japan before translation (e.g. by a sworn translator in the Netherlands).
workflow: Obtain apostille on the document from the designated Japanese authority
Have the document translated by a translator sworn in by a Dutch court if required
Submit apostilled document and translation to the requesting authority
documentLegalizationCountryExamples.note: Japan has no sworn translators for Dutch procedures; translation is typically done in the Netherlands.

exampleScenarios.title: US expat apostilling birth certificate
exampleScenarios.summary: Birth certificate for municipality registration or family visa. Get an apostille from the Secretary of State in your state, then use the document (and sworn translation if required) in the Netherlands.

exampleScenarios.title: Indian professional preparing civil documents
exampleScenarios.summary: Birth certificate, marriage certificate, or police certificate for IND or municipality. Get documents apostilled by the Ministry of External Affairs in India, then translate in the Netherlands if the document is not in an accepted language.

exampleScenarios.title: Family registering marriage in Netherlands
exampleScenarios.summary: Marriage certificate for municipality registration or partner visa. Apostille in the country where the certificate was issued, then use the document (and sworn translation if required) when registering in the Netherlands.

exampleScenarios.title: Student apostilling diploma
exampleScenarios.summary: Diploma for recognition or skilled migration. Get apostille in the country of issue, then have the document translated in the Netherlands by a sworn translator if needed.

scenariosSectionIntro: How apostille fits into different expat situations. Use the official Netherlands Worldwide page for the country that issued your document.

faq.a: An apostille is an official certificate attached to a document that confirms the authenticity of the signature and issuing authority. It allows the document to be accepted in another country that participates in the Hague Apostille Convention. In the Netherlands, apostilles are typically issued by district courts.

faq.q: Where can I get an apostille in the Netherlands?
faq.a: In the Netherlands, apostilles are issued by district courts. For documents issued abroad, apostille or legalisation is usually done in the country where the document was issued. Check Netherlands Worldwide for the correct authority for your country.

faq.q: Do I need translation as well?
faq.a: Documents not in Dutch, English, French, or German usually need sworn translation for use with Dutch authorities. Apostille and translation are separate steps; you may need both. The order (apostille first or translation first) depends on the issuing country.

Common documents that often require apostille when used in the Netherlands include birth certificates, marriage certificates, divorce certificates, diplomas, police certificates, and notarial documents—especially when used for visas, residence permits, municipality registration, or other official procedures.

Processing time varies by country and authority, from same day to several days or a few weeks. Government fees are often around €20–€25 per document in the Netherlands; other countries have different fee structures. Start the process as soon as you know which documents you need.
